mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows you to manage security, compliance, and governance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies consistently across their Kubernetes clusters. Key trade-offs include the need for familiarity with policy definitions and potential performance impacts when enforcing complex policies.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

```

> This is the official repository for Kyverno.

steps.title: Install Kyverno CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Kyverno to function properly.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/v1.17.1/config/release/kyverno-crds.yaml

```

> This installs the necessary CRDs for Kyverno.

steps.title: Install Kyverno using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs the Kyverno policy engine into your Kubernetes cluster using Helm.

```bash

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --version 1.17.1 --namespace kyverno --create-namespace

```

> This installs Kyverno in a dedicated namespace.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> You should see the Kyverno pods in a Running state.

steps.title: Create a sample policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's capabilities.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> This policy requires all Pods to have an 'app' label.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

steps.title: Verify the policy is working
steps.description:
This step checks if the policy you created is enforced correctly.

```bash

kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx --restart=Never

```

> This should fail due to the missing 'app' label.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno from your cluster. Be aware that any policies created will also be deleted, which may lead to data loss if they were enforcing important configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/v1.17.1/config/release/kyverno-crds.yaml

```

> This command uninstalls Kyverno from the specified namespace.
> This command removes the CRDs associated with Kyverno.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version while ensuring that your existing configurations remain intact.

```bash

kubectl get policies --all-namespaces -o yaml > kyverno-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --version 1.17.1 --namespace kyverno

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backup existing policies.
> This command upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verify that the new version is running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Label 'app' is required.'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the policy enforcing the 'app' label is working as intended.

**Fix:**
Add the required label to your Pod definition.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in the policies or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Kyverno pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Policy not enforcing as expected.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may not be applied correctly or the match criteria may not be met.

**Fix:**
Verify the policy status with 'kubectl get cpol' and ensure the match criteria are correct.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to install Kyverno: 'Error: unable to recognize ...'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error can occur if the Kubernetes version is not compatible with the Kyverno version.

**Fix:**
Ensure your Kubernetes version is >= 1.25 and matches the supported versions.

versionNotes.changes: Improved performance for policy validation, added support for new validation rules.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
